findings.description: The UserForm constructor is omitted from the Java code example in the knowledge file. The source includes the constructor `public UserForm(Map<String, Object> params)` which shows how form properties are initialized from request parameters using `params.get("phoneNo1")` etc. This demonstrates the Nablarch form initialization pattern.
findings.source_evidence: public UserForm(Map<String, Object> params) { this.user = (UserEntity) params.get("user"); this.phoneNo1 = (String) params.get("phoneNo1"); ... }

findings.description: The HTML form example is omitted. It shows the naming convention for form fields (`userForm.phoneNo1`, `userForm.phoneNo2`, `userForm.phoneNo3` and the hidden field `userForm.user.id`), which demonstrates how the HTML name attribute prefix `userForm` corresponds to the first argument of `validateAndConvertRequest("userForm", ...)` in the Action.
findings.source_evidence: <input type="text" name="userForm.phoneNo1"/> - <input type="text" name="userForm.phoneNo2"/> - <input type="text" name="userForm.phoneNo3"/>

findings.description: PascalCase class names present in the source are missing from hints: `UserForm` (primary class being illustrated), `UserEntity` (used as a nested form property with `@ValidationTarget`), and `ApplicationException` (thrown in the Action code example when validation fails).
findings.source_evidence: public class UserForm { ... private UserEntity user; ... throw new ApplicationException(result.getMessages());
